interviewDossier.title: E Corp — Staff SWE | Interview Dossier
interviewDossier.markdown:
# E Corp — Staff SWE | Interview Dossier

**Role:** Staff Software Engineer, Platform Intelligence — build AI-powered incident triage and agentic approval workflows for a 340-service financial platform. Staff IC, remote US.

**Outcome:** Offer extended 2026-06-24; decision pending.

---

## Live cue card

**The role in one sentence:** Lead AI-augmented incident automation and developer tooling at E Corp — the kind of RAG + agentic work I've already shipped in production, at 10x the scale.

**Why I fit (say this early):**
- Meridian RAG: 4M-page corpus, sub-60s triage, 200 daily users, ~60% research-cycle reduction
- Meridian agentic workflow: Claude API + custom tools, human-in-the-loop safety, 87% procurement admin cut
- Pulsar: 3 concurrent enterprise deployments, all on schedule, zero P1s, avg ttv 14→9 weeks

**Comp anchor:** Target $240K base + $15K sign-on. Floor $190K — not a negotiating position.

---

## Key questions + grounded answers

**1. Walk me through a system you built that reduced operational toil.**

Meridian RAG pipeline: replaced a 3-hour manual research triage process with sub-60s cited-excerpt queries over a 4M-page internal corpus. Prototype to production in 8 weeks; 200+ daily internal users; approximately 60% reduction in research-cycle time. Key technical unlock: sliding-window chunking + parent-document retrieval — naive sentence-level chunking lost cross-document context and caused hallucinated citations in the early build. Fixed that, shipped the working version, added a citation-verification suite to catch future regressions.

**2. How would you design an LLM-powered incident triage pipeline for a high-volume financial platform?**

Index runbooks, past postmortems, and service topology docs in a vector store — pgvector on existing Postgres to start, HNSW index for sub-500ms retrieval. On alert: extract structured context (service, error class, affected metrics), run semantic search, retrieve top-k chunks, pass with alert context to an LLM, produce a structured triage card (probable cause, severity reasoning, recommended first action, escalation path). Deliver to on-call in Slack in under 10 seconds. Human reviews and acts; the AI layer is strictly read-only and additive — never in the alert delivery path, never auto-executing on write actions.

**3. Tell me about aligning non-technical stakeholders on a risky AI feature.**

Meridian agentic procurement: finance team's default was "no" — they'd seen automation projects fail silently before. I didn't ask for permission. I ran a live demo on 10 of their own already-resolved procurement requests, showed the agent's draft email and budget-code lookup, then showed the human-in-the-loop confirmation step before any send. I invited them to find errors. They found two: ambiguous budget code mappings. We fixed those live. By the end of the session the finance manager asked to expand the pilot. Observable system plus an explicit human gate turns "do you trust AI?" into "do you trust your own judgment?" They always do.

**4. How do you evaluate retrieval quality without a labeled test set?**

Three layers: offline gold-standard (30-50 known-good scenarios, run on a schedule after every corpus update — catches regressions before they hit production); implicit in-production feedback (did the on-call act on the suggestion within 5 minutes, and did they click through on the cited source?); explicit feedback (thumbs-up/down on the triage card in Slack — even 10% response rate on high-sev alerts catches systematic gaps). At Meridian this combo caught a deprecated procurement policy doc that kept surfacing in retrieval; fixed the corpus hygiene, not the model.

**5. How do you mentor as a staff IC without formal management authority?**

Be the person they show the design to before the PR, not after. Office-hours 1:1s where they drive — they bring the problem, I ask questions. Most of the time the person already has 80% of the right answer and needs a thinking partner, not an authority figure. I externalize my own design reasoning: short design docs, "why" comments in code rather than "what." If I get the same category of question twice, I build the document or pattern that handles it — not another 1:1 answer. The goal is to remove myself as the bottleneck, not become one.

---

## Questions I asked

1. What does success look like at 6 months — what's shipped and what's measurably better?
2. Is this team starting greenfield, or inheriting existing infrastructure I'd own from day one?

---

## Study notes (pre-onsite skim)

- E Corp AI-ops initiative is greenfield within a mature infra org. Public SRE blog mentions "zero-ticket incident response" as the north star — tie answers to that.
- Tech stack pointers: pgvector + HNSW for retrieval; Claude or OpenAI API for completion; Python primary in the team.
- Human-in-the-loop pattern: draft → confirm → execute. Never auto-execute write-side actions (email sends, infra changes, permission grants).
- Alert deduplication: (service_name, error_class) keyed sliding window; count is the severity proxy, not noise volume.
- Eval harness: offline gold-standard regression suite + implicit in-production feedback + periodic explicit thumbs-up/down. Run offline suite on a schedule after every corpus update or prompt change.
- Distributed systems concepts: multi-region failover, HNSW approximate nearest-neighbor, p99 latency at burst load, graceful degradation when AI layer is unavailable (always additive, never in the critical path).

interviewDossier.title: Cyberdyne Systems — Staff ML Engineer · Onsite Prep
interviewDossier.markdown:
## Cyberdyne Systems — Staff ML Engineer · Onsite Prep
**The role in one line:** Own model-serving reliability and evaluation pipelines for autonomous systems — production ML conscience of the neural-net division.

---

### Likely questions + grounded answers

**Q: Walk me through a production ML system you owned end-to-end.**
Pulsar scheduling AI — owned the full deployment lifecycle across 3 concurrent enterprise accounts in one quarter. Discovery, integration (Salesforce, Greenhouse, Workday), config, go-live, hypercare. Zero P1 incidents. Cut avg time-to-value 14→9 weeks. The metric was reliability, not model novelty.

**Q: How do you design an eval pipeline for high-stakes model outputs?**
Meridian RAG pipeline over 4M pages: eval was recall + citation precision, human-review gate on any excerpt driving downstream action. Offline eval gives coverage; online sampling tells you if the model is still behaving in prod. For autonomous systems I'd add shadow scoring + confidence gating before any write action.

**Q: Tell me about a time you stopped or delayed a deployment.**
Pulsar, mid-quarter: customer pushed an undocumented schema change. Model eval on new format untested — pulled go-live by one week, ran evals, re-confirmed with customer. Pre-agreed readiness criteria gave me cover to hold without a political fight.

**Q: How do you handle safety vs. product disagreement on a release?**
Set stop criteria before the model is done, not during launch review. At Pulsar I brought readiness criteria into the project kickoff; a hold becomes 'enforcing what we agreed,' not 'engineer slowing things down.' Safety veto should be structural.

**Q: What's your read on agentic systems in production?**
Shipped one at Meridian: Claude API + custom tools parsing vendor quotes, ERP budget lookups, drafting approval emails with human-in-the-loop confirmation before writes. ~87% reduction in procurement admin. Confidence gate on every tool call; full audit log; human step non-negotiable until 3 months of prod data.

---

### Comp anchor
Band $215–255K base / $320–380K TC (posted). Ask $240K+ — hybrid SF is a real concession for an Austin-remote candidate; price it in.

### Questions to ask
1. Who has stop-deploy authority — engineering, safety, or product — and what happens when they disagree?
2. What does the eval pipeline look like today, and what's the biggest coverage gap?

---

### Study layer
- **Model-serving:** batching (dynamic, request coalescing), canary deploys, P50/P99 latency budgets, rollback automation
- **Eval patterns:** offline evals, shadow scoring, confidence-gate thresholds, outcome feedback loops, human-in-the-loop review
- **Safety context:** Cyberdyne's layered review process for models touching physical/operational systems; understand OKR cycle overlap between safety and infra

conversations.notes: 30-min intro. Role is hybrid SF, 2 fixed days/week. Band $215–255K confirmed. Riley flagged $230K+ ask; Priya said within range. Advancing to technical screen.
60-min deep dive on model-serving latency, batching, and observability. Walked through Pulsar deploy reliability work. Strong signal; advancing to system design.

interviewDossier.title: Massive Dynamic — Staff Engineer
interviewDossier.markdown:
## Massive Dynamic — Staff Engineer
**Panel Interview | 2026-07-01 | Remote (Zoom) | 10 AM–1 PM CT**

---

### Live cue card

**Role in one line:** Staff Engineer on the AI Platform team — own the LLM integration layer; team-lead duties; cross-functional with data science and enterprise customers.

**Q: Walk us through the most complex distributed system you've designed.**
> Meridian RAG pipeline over 4M pages. Key decisions: 512-token chunks + 64-token overlap, hybrid BM25+dense retrieval, cross-encoder reranker top-20→top-5. Built eval harness (LLM-as-judge + human spot-check) before shipping. Prototype→prod in 8 weeks; 200+ daily users; ~60% research-cycle-time reduction.

**Q: How do you handle multi-tenant data isolation in a shared LLM platform?**
> Namespace-level corpus separation per tenant. Metadata filtering applied *before* retrieval (not post-filter — that risks internal leakage). Audit log every retrieval: tenant_id, query hash, retrieved doc_ids. Rate-limit at API gateway, not inside the model call.

**Q: Describe leading a cross-functional initiative without formal authority.**
> Pulsar Q1 2026: 3 concurrent enterprise deployments, 4 customer eng teams + internal infra. Shared status board, weekly 15-min standups, escalation ladder documented upfront. All 3 live on schedule; 0 P1 incidents. Time-to-value 14→9 weeks.

**Q: How do you evaluate retrieval quality continuously?**
> Three layers: (1) offline eval on 500+ labeled query/doc pairs (Recall@5, MRR@5); (2) online A/B on live traffic (click-through, session length); (3) LLM-as-judge sampling 50 queries/day. Regression gate in CI: PR that drops Recall@5 by >2 points blocks deploy.

**Q: Where do you want to be in 3 years?**
> Staff or Principal, still hands-on, owning architecture of a platform multiple product teams build on. Player-coach, not full EM. The best version has me in customer conversations once a quarter and writing production code the rest of the time.

**Comp anchor:** Floor $215K base. Ask $240K. Posted band $215–255K — targeting mid-range; use TC ($320–380K) as the headline number with the panel.

**2 questions to ask:**
1. "What's the biggest gap between where the LLM integration layer is today and where the roadmap needs it in 12 months?"
2. "How does Staff Eng interface with enterprise customers — do you join discovery or onboarding calls?"

---

### Study layer

**What Riley brings:** Meridian RAG (production, 200+ users, 8-week build) + Pulsar FDE (enterprise deployment lifecycle, 3 concurrent) gives a rare combo of platform depth + customer-facing instinct.

**Gap to address proactively:** Marcus probed platform-side depth in the HM screen — lean into the Meridian pipeline architecture when the panel pushes on infra depth. If asked about ACL change handling: "I'd tighten the async cache TTL to synchronous invalidation on write for compliance-critical changes — the 60-second window is a tradeoff worth closing given the firm's compliance posture."

**Watch for:** Role is more inward-facing than Riley's FDE targets. Acknowledge honestly — the quarterly enterprise BRs are the feedback loop Riley would lean on.

**Artifact:** workspace/interview-packets/demo/massive-dynamic-panel.md

conversations.notes: 30-min call. Role owns LLM integration layer, some enterprise customer touchpoints. Confirmed fully remote. Comp band $215–255K base / $320–380K TC. Advanced to HM screen.
45-min. Covered RAG pipeline at Meridian + 3 concurrent deployments at Pulsar. Marcus liked the FDE angle; probed platform-side depth. Advanced to panel interview.

followUp.note: Prep for the upcoming Massive Dynamic panel (Jul 1).

communications.subject: Forward Deployed Engineer — offer and negotiation
communications.summary: Recruiter inbound → loop → written offer ($182K base) → counter ($190K) → Aperture final ($185K) — below floor. Riley withdrew amicably.

messages.subject: Forward Deployed Engineer — Aperture Science
messages.summary: Jordan Marsh reached out; remote FDE role for Enrichment Center enterprise deployments. Proposed 20-min intro call.

messages.subject: Re: Forward Deployed Engineer — Aperture Science
messages.summary: Riley replied with interest, flagged $190K base target upfront, proposed Thursday 2pm CT. Jordan confirmed and noted top of band is $190K.

messages.subject: Thank you — Aperture Science FDE loop
messages.summary: Nadia (panel lead) thanked Riley after tech deep dive; cited deployment pipeline and write-gate answers as standouts. Offer coming.

messages.subject: Aperture Science — offer letter
messages.summary: Aperture written offer: $182K base, 48K options, 10% bonus, $10K signing. Jordan noted below target but above midpoint. Expires June 19.

messages.subject: Re: Aperture Science — offer letter
messages.summary: Riley countered in writing: $190K base (floor) to move forward; everything else acceptable. Offered to jump on a call. Waiting for response.

messages.subject: Re: Aperture Science — offer letter
messages.summary: Aperture came back at $185K — best they can do. Door stays open if priorities change. Below the $190K floor; Riley deciding.

messages.subject: Re: Aperture Science — offer letter (withdrawal)
messages.summary: Riley withdrew amicably; thanked the team, cited base below the $190K hard floor. No hard feelings, door left open on both sides.

communications.subject: Principal Engineer, AI Platform — Hooli
communications.summary: Marcus Webb referral → recruiter intro → HM → technical screen; Hooli rejected Riley post-technical. Dana Park sent warm rejection 2026-06-19.

messages.subject: Principal Engineer, AI Platform — Hooli (referral from Marcus Webb)
messages.summary: Dana Park cites Marcus Webb's referral and invites Riley to a 30-min intro call about the Principal Engineer, AI Platform role.

messages.subject: Re: Principal Engineer, AI Platform — Hooli (referral from Marcus Webb)
messages.summary: Riley confirms interest, proposes Thursday 5/14 or Friday 5/15, and flags target base of $265K+ upfront to avoid wasted cycles.

messages.subject: Re: Principal Engineer, AI Platform — Hooli (referral from Marcus Webb)
messages.summary: Dana confirms Thursday 5/14 11am CT and notes the posted band is $250–290K base — should align with Riley's ask.

messages.subject: Thank you — Hooli Principal Eng loop
messages.summary: Riley thanks Dana and the team for three strong rounds and calls out the technical discussion with Priya as a highlight.

messages.subject: Re: Thank you — Hooli Principal Eng loop
messages.summary: Dana thanks Riley for three strong rounds; team went another direction. Warm close, Hooli keeping the profile on file.

communications.subject: Staff Engineer, Spaceframe — Weyland-Yutani (remote, $215–255K base)
communications.summary: Priya Vasquez outreach → screen May 24 → technical Jun 7 → HM Jun 11 → final panel Jun 14 → rejected Jun 19. Narrow internal calibration, no offer.

messages.subject: Staff Engineer, Spaceframe — Weyland-Yutani (remote, $215–255K base)
messages.summary: Priya Vasquez (W-Y recruiter) reached out after finding Riley's profile. HITL workflow and RAG pipeline experience flagged as strong fit. Invited to 30-min screen.

messages.subject: Re: Staff Engineer, Spaceframe — Weyland-Yutani (remote, $215–255K base)
messages.summary: Riley expressed interest, noted Meridian HITL pattern as direct fit, requested JD and offered Thu/Fri availability for a call.

messages.subject: Re: Staff Engineer, Spaceframe — JD + screen scheduling
messages.summary: Priya sent the JD and booked the recruiter screen for May 24 at 10 AM CT. Pre-flagged on-call (~1 wk/quarter) and ~10% travel to confirm no blockers.

messages.subject: Re: Staff Engineer, Spaceframe — JD + screen scheduling
messages.summary: Riley confirmed May 24 screen, said on-call and travel are not blockers but wants to understand the escalation tree during the process.

messages.subject: Weyland-Yutani Final Loop — invitation + logistics
messages.summary: Strong feedback from tech and HM screens. Final-loop invite: Jun 14 4-panel (Lena Park, Soren Holt, Kira Oduya, Dag Eriksen). Debrief by Jun 16.

messages.subject: Re: Weyland-Yutani Final Loop — invitation + logistics
messages.summary: Riley confirmed Jun 14. Flagged intent to raise on-call scope and safety-escalation ownership with Dag and Lena in the loop.

messages.subject: Re: Weyland-Yutani Final Loop — thank you
messages.summary: Riley thanked all 4 panelists, noted strong energy throughout, and flagged on-call escalation ownership as the one open thread to resolve.

messages.subject: Re: Weyland-Yutani Final Loop — decision update
messages.summary: Priya: no offer — narrow internal calibration on ownership scope went the other way. Panel feedback was strong across all 4 interviewers.

communications.subject: Staff ML Engineer at Cyberdyne — quick intro call?
communications.summary: Applied 2026-05-15; Priya Desai reached out 2026-05-17. Recruiter screen 2026-05-19 confirmed hybrid SF and $215–255K band. Technical screen with Marcus Webb 2026-05-26 and system design with Dr. Kazan 2026-06-02 both advanced. Final onsite invite received 2026-06-22 for June 28.

messages.subject: Application Received — Staff ML Engineer at Cyberdyne Systems
messages.summary: Auto-confirmation of Riley's board application. Recruiting team will follow up in 5–7 business days if there's a match.

messages.subject: Staff ML Engineer at Cyberdyne — quick intro call?
messages.summary: Priya flagged Pulsar + Meridian as a strong fit. Disclosed hybrid SF 2 days/week and $215–255K band up front; asked for a 30-min intro call.

messages.subject: Re: Staff ML Engineer at Cyberdyne — quick intro call?
messages.summary: Riley confirmed Tuesday 5/19 at 10am PT, noted Austin base and $230K+ ask given the SF hybrid concession.

messages.subject: Re: Staff ML Engineer — next step: technical screen with Marcus Webb
messages.summary: Positive screen feedback. Priya confirmed $230K+ ask is within band; scheduled 60-min technical screen with Marcus Webb for May 26.

messages.subject: Re: Staff ML Engineer — next step: system design with Dr. Kazan
messages.summary: Marcus gave strong feedback on the technical screen. Advancing to 60-min system design with Dr. Sarah Kazan (ML Platform Lead) on June 2.

messages.subject: Cyberdyne final onsite — invitation for June 28
messages.summary: Dr. Kazan gave strong system design feedback. Final onsite confirmed June 28: coding, HM, system design debrief, and safety panel.

communications.subject: Staff Engineer, AI Platform — Massive Dynamic
communications.summary: Camille Torres reached out via alumni referral; recruiter screen Jun 16, HM screen with Marcus Webb Jun 23. Panel scheduled 2026-07-01.

messages.subject: Staff Engineer, AI Platform — Massive Dynamic (referred by alumni)
messages.summary: Camille reaches out via alumni referral; role owns LLM integration layer, team-lead duties, $215–255K base. Invited to 30-min intro call.

messages.subject: Re: Staff Engineer, AI Platform — Massive Dynamic (referred by alumni)
messages.summary: Riley confirms interest and offers Thursday 6/12 or Friday 6/13 availability. Flags $240K base target upfront.

messages.subject: Re: Staff Engineer, AI Platform — great talking with you!
messages.summary: Post-recruiter-screen follow-up. Camille confirms strong fit signal and schedules 45-min HM screen with Marcus Webb for Jun 23 at 11 AM CT.

messages.subject: Panel Interview Invitation — Massive Dynamic | 2026-07-01
messages.summary: Marcus gave strong fit signal on platform depth and FDE. Panel invite for Jul 1 (10 AM CT): Jordan Reyes, Alicia Morrow, Marcus Webb.

messages.subject: Re: Panel Interview Invitation — Massive Dynamic | 2026-07-01
messages.summary: Riley confirms the Jul 1 panel and thanks Camille. Will watch for Zoom links before the weekend.

communications.subject: Your application for Senior Software Engineer at Pied Piper
communications.summary: ATS auto-rejection after apply; no interview rounds — application did not advance.

messages.subject: Your application for Senior Software Engineer at Pied Piper
messages.summary: Generic ATS notice: Pied Piper will not be moving forward with Riley's application; no feedback provided.

communications.subject: Your application for Senior Infrastructure Engineer at Umbrella Corporation
communications.summary: ATS form rejection 2026-06-10, no interview — applied 2026-05-29, never advanced.

messages.subject: Your application for Senior Infrastructure Engineer at Umbrella Corporation
messages.summary: Generic ATS rejection — thanked Riley for applying, role filled with another candidate, encouraged future applications.

communications.subject: Your application to Initech
communications.summary: ATS form rejection from Initech for Software Engineer — no interview, 12 days after applying.

messages.subject: Your application to Initech
messages.summary: Generic ATS rejection — Initech thanks Riley for applying to Software Engineer but is moving forward with other candidates.

communications.subject: Your application — Senior Software Engineer at Spectre
communications.summary: ATS form rejection from Spectre — applied 2026-05-29 via recruiter, no interview, declined 2026-06-10.

messages.subject: Your application — Senior Software Engineer at Spectre
messages.summary: Generic ATS rejection: Spectre thanks Riley for applying but is moving forward with other candidates.

sourced.note: Found via network; no open req yet — reach out to the lab director about an Applied AI seat before the next resonance cascade. Remote and on-target comp.
Primary-bucket role right in town — NY hybrid, no relo. Warm intro possible through the network. 'What do we know? We don't know.'
